# RKE2 Cluster Provisioning

We use the Terraform Harvester provider to create the virtual machines and `cloud-init` to perform the installation of RKE2 and its configuration files. There are a few minor edits on the hosts needed before we can turn the services on.

## Generate configuration files

All of the configuration files are stored in the `files/` subdirectory. A script is provided that creates `cloud-config` directives to place these files onto the guest filesystems. Additionally, it will create a service account and token in the cluster namespace with the required role bindings for the `harvester-cloud-provider` and also write a kubeconfig file for this service account.

First, create the namespace:

```sh
kubectl create ns kasm
```

Now run the script:

```sh
./scripts/generate-cloud-config.sh harvester-cloud-provider kasm
```

It will print out something like this:

```console
$ ./scripts/generate-config.sh
write_files:
  - encoding: b64
    content: 
    path: /etc/rancher/rke2/audit-policy.yaml
  - encoding: b64
    content: 
    path: /etc/rancher/rke2/config.yaml
  - encoding: b64
    content: 
    path: /etc/rancher/rke2/rancher-pss.yaml
  - encoding: b64
    content: bWlycm9yczoKICAiKiI6CiAgICBlbmRwb2ludDoKICAgICAgLSAiaHR0cHM6Ly9yZWdpc3RyeS5sb2NhbGRvbWFpbjo1MDAwIg==
    path: /etc/rancher/rke2/registries.yaml
  - encoding: b64
    content: YXBpVmVyc2lvbjogaGVsbS5jYXR0bGUuaW8vdjEKa2luZDogSGVsbUNoYXJ0Q29uZmlnCm1ldGFkYXRhOgogIG5hbWU6IGhhcnZlc3Rlci1jbG91ZC1wcm92aWRlcgogIG5hbWVzcGFjZToga3ViZS1zeXN0ZW0Kc3BlYzoKICB2YWx1ZXNDb250ZW50OiB8LQogICAgZ2xvYmFsOgogICAgICBjYXR0bGU6CiAgICAgICAgY2x1c3Rlck5hbWU6IGthc20=
    path: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/manifests/harvester-cloud-provider-config.yaml
  - encoding: b64
    content: 
    path: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/manifests/rke2-cilium-config.yaml
  - encoding: b64
    content: 
    path: /var/lib/rancher/rke2/server/manifests/rke2-traefik-config.yaml
```

To verify the service account and token were created:

```console
$ kubectl get sa -n kasm
NAME                       SECRETS   AGE
default                    0         7m
harvester-cloud-provider   0         5m47s
$ kubectl get secret -n kasm harvester-cloud-provider-token -ogo-template='{{.data.token | base64decode}}' | jwt decode -

Token header
------------
{
  "alg": "RS256",
  "kid": "uUP-f4_yYnjxUpnvCfonFG8miU1TTLkfAk0Z13espwE"
}

Token claims
------------
{
  "iss": "kubernetes/serviceaccount",
  "kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/namespace": "kasm",
  "kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/secret.name": "harvester-cloud-provider-token",
  "kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/service-account.name": "harvester-cloud-provider",
  "kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/service-account.uid": "e0fa4291-04b8-42d7-a48f-ecc5ad423c1a",
  "sub": "system:serviceaccount:kasm:harvester-cloud-provider"
}
```

There is already a `ClusterRole` for the cloud provider built into Harvester. This script creates only the binding:

```console
$ kubectl get clusterrolebinding harvester-cloud-provider
NAME                       ROLE                                        AGE
harvester-cloud-provider   ClusterRole/harvesterhci.io:cloudprovider   7d22h
```

## Create cluster node VMs

Run the provided example Terraform or provision in whichever way pleases you. The provided Terraform module create 3 nodes with 4 cores, 8GiB of RAM, 40GiB of storage, adds the CA cert of my homelab to the trust store, and creates `iptables` rules to prevent Internet access. If used, you will want to adapt this to your environment. Note that the example does not use the `ca_certs` module for `cloud-init` because it is not verified for Rocky and will not run.

`ssh` to the first node and start RKE2:

```sh
sudo systemctl enable --now rke2-server
```

Optionally, watch the logs to see it start up:

```sh
journalctl -fu rke2-server
```
